content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 17,
1992
Lance H.
Olson
Olson,
Connelly,
Hagel,
Fang
& Leidigh
Capitol Bank Center
300 Capitol Mall,
Suite 350
Sacramento,
CA
95814
RE:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92—545
Dear Mr.
Olson:
You have requested advice on behalf of the Yes on 167
Committee—Californians for a Recovering Economy,
Sponsored by the
California Tax Reform Association for which you serve as
treasurer,
concerning reporting the use of a 900—number marketing
program as a means of receiving contributions under the Political
Reform Act
(the “Act”).l/
QUESTIONS
1.
With respect to the telephone charges incurred by the
callers:
a.
Are these charges contributions?
b.
If the charges are contributions,
what is the date
of the contribution for reporting purposes?
c.
Since the average call will be two minutes, for a
total charge of $6.00,
may the Yes on 167 Committee assume for
reporting and recordkeeping purposes that the individual
contributions are under $25?
d.
How should the total amount of the contributions be
calculated?
Should the amounts withheld by Interactive TeleMedia
Services Corporation (“Interactive)
for charge backs and credits
be included?
Should the amounts withheld by Interactive for its
fees and charges be included?
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95812
•
(91o) 322-5660


Lance H.
Olson
Our File Number A—92-545
Page
2
2.
With respect to the program expenditures:
a.
How should the retention of a percentage of the
proceeds by Interactive for charge backs and credits be reported
by the Yes on
167 Committee?
If these are reportable payments,
what are the reportable payments,
what are the dates of the
payments?
b.
How should the Yes on 167 Committee report the other
Interactive charges and fees which are retained by Interactive out
of the gross proceeds?
If these are reportable payments, what are
the dates of the payments?
c.
Is there any required subvendor reporting in these
circumstances?
If yes,
what type of reporting is required and
what types of efforts are required to obtain the subvendor
information?
3.
What records is the Yes on 167 Committee required to
keep in connection with this program?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
a.
The charges made to the 900 telephone number are
contributions to the Yes on 167 Committee.
b.
The contributions are considered received by the Yes
on
167 Committee on the date Interactive receives the funds from
the telephone carrier.
c.
The Committee may assume for reporting and
recordkeeping purposes that the individual contributions are less
than $25.00 since the average call will be two minutes in duration
for a total charge of $6.00 per call.
Under the facts presented,
persons who wish to make additional contributions will leave their
names and addresses and will be contacted by the Committee at a
later date.
d.
The amount of the contribution is the $6.00 or the
total amount collected from the caller.
Amounts withheld by
Interactive for charges and fees should not be deducted from the
amount of the contribution and must be reported as expenditures on
Schedule
E by the Yes on 167 Committee.
2.
a.,
b.
Amounts withheld by Interactive for charges and
fees must be reported on Schedule
E as expenditures by the Yes on
167
Committee at the time Interactive deducts the fees.


Lance H.
Olson
Our File Number A-92-545
Page
3
c.
The Yes on 167 Committee is required to report
expenditures made on its behalf to the telephone carrier, which is
a subvendor of Interactive.
3.
The Yes on 167 Committee is required to maintain records
of each contribution received and expenditure made of $25.00 or
more.
Contributions received and expenditures made of less than
$25.00 should be recorded in a lump sum for each day.
The 1992/93
Campaign Disclosure Manual
D provides guidelines for a
recordkeeping system that will assist the Committee in maintaining
adequate records.
In addition, the Committee will need to retain
in its records the contract, service agreement and any other legal
documents entered into between the Committee and Interactive.
FACTS
The Yes on 167 Committee is primarily formed to support the
passage of Proposition 167 on the November 3,
1992,
statewide
ballot.
The Yes on 167 Committee entered into a contact with
Interactive TeleMedia Services Corporation which is in the
business of providing interactive telephone call processing
services to its customers in the conduct of 900 number marketing
programs.
The contract between the Yes on 167 Committee and
Interactive generally provides for the instituting of a
“900”
telephone line service as a means by which persons may contribute
to the Committee.
Under the contract, the Committee provides the
TV commercial script and the telephone script.
Interactive
establishes the 900 number line with the telephone carrier,
maintains the equipment,
and retrieves data from the callers.
The 900 number program works as follows.
The TV viewer views
the ad and places a call to the 900 number.
The caller listens to
an 18—second message which states the phone charges and provides
an opportunity for the caller to hang up without incurring any
charges.
If the caller stays on the line, he/she will listen to a
short message about Proposition 167.
Callers may leave their name
and address if they wish to make further contributions.
The cost
of the call is $5.00 for the first minute,
and $1.00 for each
additional minute.
The charges to the caller are billed on the
caller’s next regular phone bill.
The telephone carrier collects
the charges with the payment of the telephone bill and forwards
the money to Interactive.
Interactive retains ten percent of the
“gross call proceeds” to cover charge backs and credits,
which
callers may request or be entitled to.
In addition, Interactive
will deduct its fees and charges.
Interactive will then forward
the balance of the proceeds to the Yes on 167 Committee.
This


Lance H.
Olson
Our File Number A-92-545
Page
4
payment will be made 75 days after the end of the month when the
call occurred.
Interactive will provide to the Yes on 167
committee daily activity reports on the daily call volume data for
each 900 number.
In addition, Interactive will provide to the Yes
on 167 Committee on a periodic basis, the data derived from the
transcription of the callers’ messages.
Pursuant to a telephone conversation on October 22,
1992,
with Carla Wardlow,
Ms.
Diane Fishburn,
an attorney with your
firm,
indicated that certain 900 companies will advance to clients
some portion of the funds they anticipate raising.
However,
Ms.
Fishburn stated that the Committee did not receive such an advance
from Interactive.
ANALYSIS
The Commission has previously issued written advice on the
issue of soliciting contributions using a tele—network
organization’s 900-number.
(Buck-Walsh Advice Letter No.
1-90-
239,
copy enclosed.)
The advice provided in Buck-Walsh states
that the collection of contributions by means of a tele—network
using a telephone company as a collection agent is not prohibited
under the Act provided the procedures for solicitation and
reporting of contributions comply with the requirements of the
Act.
Buck-Walsh provides that the full amount charged to the
caller is the amount of the contribution to the campaign
committee.
The Yes on 167 Committee must report the total charge to the
caller as a contribution.
Based on the facts presented above, the
average call will be two minutes for a total charge of $6.00 per
call.
The Committee must report $6.00 as the contribution.
The date the contribution is received by the Yes on 167
Committee is the date that the Committee or agent of the Committee
obtains possession or control of the check or other negotiable
instrument by which the contribution is made.
Based on the facts
presented above, the Committee must report the contributions
received on the date Interactive receives the payments from the
telephone carrier.
(FPPC Regulation 18421.1.)


Lance H.
Olson
Our File Number A-92-545
Page
5
For reporting and recordkeeping purposes, the Yes on 167
Committee may assume that the individual contributions are less
than $25.00.
Section 84104 requires treasurers of committees to
“maintain such detailed accounts, records, bills and receipts that
are necessary to prepare campaign statements. . . .“
Regulation
18401 clarifies that for a contribution received or other receipt
of less than $25,
or an expenditure made of less than $25:
• . . records shall contain a continuous computation
of campaign account balances,
and also shall
include a listing reflecting the dates and daily
totals of such contributions, other receipts, or
expenditures on the dates of such contributions,
other receipts or expenditures.
Buck-Walsh further provides that all costs associated with
the collection is an expenditure of the campaign committee and
must be reported by the committee.
Section 84303 requires committees to report expenditures,
other than normal overhead and normal operating expenses,
made on
their behalf by agents or independent contractors in the same
detail as if such expenditures were made directly by the
committee.
Section 84303 requires disclosure of expenditures made
by an agent or independent contractor for services which are not
routinely provided by the agent or independent contractor.
Regulation 18431 provides,
in relevant part,:
The following expenditures,
and all similar
expenditures, when made by an agent or
independent contractor,
including any vendor
or subvendor,
on behalf of or for the benefit
of a candidate or committee, must be reported
under Government Code Section 84303....
Expenditures which must be reported include:
(a) (3) (F)
Any expenditures to any person who transfers
funds to any other person for expenditures which
are reportable under this section.
(c)
An expenditure which is required to be reported
by Government Code Section 84303 must be reported by
the candidate or committee at the same time and in
the same manner and detail as required under
Government Code Section 84211 for the candidate’s
or committee’s direct expenditures.


Lance H.
Olson
Our File Number A-92-545
Page
6
Therefore, the amounts withheld by Interactive for charge
backs and credits must be reported as expenditures on Schedule E
by the Yes on 167 Committee at the time Interactive deducts the
fees.
In addition, the Yes on 167 Committee must report
expenditures made on its behalf to the telephone carrier, which is
a subvendor of Interactive.
The Yes on 167 Committee is required to maintain adequate
records of receipts and expenditures as required by Section 84104
and FPPC Regulation 18401.
In addition, the Committee should
retain copies of the contract, service agreement and any other
legal documents entered into with Interactive TeleMedia Services
Corporation.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at 916/322—5662.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By: vasager7
Politi al Reform Consultant
Enclosure
